headline: CEX with no recorded public repo or audits; on-chain contract verification status largely unconfirmed

findings.text: OKX is a centralized exchange whose core matching engine and custody are off-chain. It does deploy on-chain contracts (DEX aggregator, Proof of Reserves Merkle verifier, Web3 wallet factory) on Ethereum and other chains. The OKX DEX aggregator router on Ethereum (0x40aA958dd87FC8305b97f2BA922CDdCa374bcd7F) is visible on Etherscan and its source code appears verified, but this covers only a narrow slice of OKX's overall operation. No canonical list of all deployed contract addresses was confirmed during this run.

protocol.github is recorded as '(none recorded)'. No canonical public GitHub organisation for OKX's on-chain contract source code was confirmed during this run. OKX has a GitHub organisation (github.com/okx) with some public repositories (e.g. proof-of-reserves tooling), but none is linked as the canonical source for the deployed exchange or DEX contracts, and no commit SHA was pinned.

protocol.audit_links is recorded as '(none recorded)'. No audit report URLs were provided or discovered during this run for the core OKX on-chain contracts. OKX has published blog posts referencing third-party security reviews of its Proof of Reserves system (mentioning firms such as Hacken and Armanino), but no direct PDF or report URL was verified in this run.

Because no audit report URL was confirmed (V3), auditor recognition cannot be assessed. References to Hacken (a recognized firm) reviewing OKX PoR exist in OKX blog posts, but the actual report was not retrieved and verified during this run.
Post-audit drift cannot be assessed because no audited commit or report was confirmed (V3/V4). The gap between any historical audit and the current deployment state is therefore unknown.

The OKX DEX aggregator router on Ethereum is a non-proxy contract according to its Etherscan page, so proxy/implementation split is not applicable for that specific contract. For other OKX contracts (wallet factories, PoR contracts) the proxy/implementation status was not confirmed during this run.

steelman.red: OKX has no recorded public GitHub repo for its core contracts and no recorded audit links, satisfying the rubric's explicit red conditions; even the on-chain DEX router represents only a tiny fraction of OKX's overall custodial and trading infrastructure, which is entirely opaque.
steelman.orange: OKX's DEX aggregator source code appears verified on Etherscan, its Proof of Reserves open-source tooling is published at github.com/okx/proof-of-reserves, and third-party PoR attestations from recognized firms have been referenced publicly, suggesting partial but meaningful verifiability for the subset of on-chain components.
steelman.green: If OKX's DEX aggregator and PoR contracts are all individually source-verified on-chain, backed by a public repo, and covered by recognized-firm audits (Hacken, etc.), the on-chain footprint could meet green criteria for those specific contracts — though the CEX core would remain off-chain and unauditable.

rationale.verdict: Choosing red because the two explicit red conditions are both met: no public GitHub repo is recorded for the protocol (protocol.github = none recorded), and no audit links are recorded (protocol.audit_links = none recorded). While OKX does have some on-chain contracts with apparent source verification (e.g. the DEX aggregator router on Etherscan) and a partial public repo for PoR tooling (github.com/okx/proof-of-reserves), these were not confirmed with pinned commit SHAs or retrieved audit reports during this run, and they cover only a narrow slice of OKX's overall operation. The absence of a canonical contract source repo and confirmed audit coverage for the deployed contracts drives the red grade.

evidence.shows: OKX DEX aggregator router contract on Ethereum — source code appears verified on Etherscan, non-proxy contract
OKX public GitHub repository for Proof of Reserves open-source tooling; demonstrates partial public repo presence but is not the canonical source for exchange or DEX contracts
OKX Proof of Reserves transparency page referencing third-party auditors and Merkle tree verification; no direct audit PDF link confirmed

unknowns: V1: Full canonical list of all OKX deployed contract addresses across all supported chains was not confirmed; only the Ethereum DEX aggregator router was spot-checked on Etherscan
V2: No commit SHA was pinned for any OKX contract source repo; github.com/okx/proof-of-reserves exists but was not matched to a specific deployed contract bytecode
V3: No direct audit report PDF or URL was retrieved and verified during this run; OKX blog references to Hacken and Armanino PoR reviews were noted but not opened
V4: Auditor identity and recognition cannot be confirmed without a retrieved audit report (blocked by V3)
V5: Post-audit drift cannot be assessed without a confirmed audited commit (blocked by V3/V4)
V6: Proxy/implementation status of OKX wallet factory and PoR verifier contracts on Ethereum and other chains was not confirmed during this run

protocol_metadata.about: OKX is a centralized cryptocurrency exchange offering spot, derivatives, and options trading across hundreds of digital assets for retail and institutional users globally. It also operates an embedded Web3 wallet and DEX aggregator that routes trades across decentralized liquidity sources on Ethereum, Solana, and other chains. OKX publishes a Merkle-tree-based Proof of Reserves to allow users to verify their balances are held 1:1 on-chain. It is one of the largest exchanges by volume and distinguishes itself through deep cross-chain liquidity aggregation and a self-custody wallet integrated directly into its app.
